literal: the doctrine of "in some respect"; from syāt ("from a certain standpoint; in some respect") + vāda ("doctrine")
invariant_definition: The Jaina discipline of conditioned predication, usually presented as the sevenfold scheme (*sapta-bhaṅgī*). It is the formal counterpart of *anekānta-vāda*: because an existent has many aspects, assertion must state the respect under which it speaks. The indeclinable *syāt* does that work. It does not mean bare possibility in the modern modal sense; it means "from a certain standpoint," "in some respect," or "under a certain condition." On that basis the tradition permits seven exhaustive predicative forms: affirmation, negation, affirmation-and-negation under different respects, and combinations involving *avaktavya* or inexpressibility when incompatible predicates are being forced into one unmarked assertion. The scheme is therefore not a license for contradiction. Its task is to preserve fine-grained realism about substances and modes while preventing unqualified claims from masquerading as total descriptions. In Jaina logic, *syād-vāda* belongs with *naya* analysis, not with a generalized skepticism about truth.

per_school.definition: Śaṅkara presents the Jaina doctrine under the heading of `saptabhaṅgī-naya` and rejects it on the ground that mutually opposed dharmas such as `sat` and `asat` cannot coherently inhere in one `dharmin`, so the anekāntika attribution of existence, non-existence, unity, plurality, permanence, and impermanence is held to be `asaṃgata`. cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.33-36 He then extends the refutation to the Jaina account of the `jīva`, arguing that a body-measured self subject to contraction and expansion would incur `akārtsnya`, `vikāra`, and finally an inability to distinguish liberated from non-liberated condition. cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.33-36

Rāmānuja sets out the Jaina sevenfold predication beginning with `syād asti`, `syān nāsti`, and `syād avaktavyam`, and then denies that one `vastu` can bear such mutually opposed determinations at once, since `viruddha-dharma` cannot be established in a single substrate. cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.2.33-36 He further argues that his own account of Brahman and the world is not an anekāntika vacillation, because the relation of body and self is fixed by the `śruti`-grounded `śarīra-śarīrin` structure rather than by oscillating predication. cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.2.33-36

Madhva explicitly reports the `kṣapaṇaka` as construing the world as `sat` and `asat`, `dvaya` and `advaya`, and otherwise under the rubric of `saptabhaṅgī`, and he rejects this because a single `padārtha` cannot be fixed by mutually conflicting determinations without destroying `niyama` in cognition. cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.236-237

Nimbārka rehearses the `saptabhaṅgī` as a sequence of qualified predications grounded in the `dravya-paryāya` distinction, but rejects the doctrine because one `dravya` cannot truly sustain opposed predicates such as `asti` and `nāsti` without the impossible conjunction of `viruddha-dharma`. cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.2.33-36 He distinguishes his own `svābhāvika-bhedābheda` from such anekāntika indeterminacy by treating difference and non-difference as scripturally determined aspects of the Brahman-world relation, not as alternating predications of one item. cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.2.33-36

per_school.not_applicable_reason: The on-disk Sāṃkhya primaries examined for this pass do not treat `syād-vāda` as a technical doctrinal topic of the school, and no substantive school-position on the term was retrievable from these materials.
The Yoga Sūtra and Vyāsa-bhāṣya materials examined for this pass do not articulate a school-defining position on `syād-vāda`, which does not function there as a native technical category.
The on-disk Mādhyamaka primaries examined here do not take up `syād-vāda` as a doctrinal category internal to the school, and no substantive school-position on the term was attested in this corpus.

The Yogācāra and allied epistemological primaries examined for this pass do not present `syād-vāda` as a school-defining topic, and no direct treatment of the term was retrievable from these materials.

The on-disk Pratyabhijñā and Trika texts examined for this pass do not frame `syād-vāda` as a technical issue within the school’s own siddhānta, so no substantive school-position on the term is attested here.

per_school.definition: In Umāsvāti's formulation, apprehension proceeds by `pramāṇa` and `naya`, so the doctrinal background of later `syād-vāda` is a regulated plurality of standpoints rather than an unqualified alternation of predicates. cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.6 The enumeration of `naya`s provides the architecture within which qualified predication is distributed, while the warning that failure to distinguish `sat` and `asat` leads to discourse 'like a madman' marks the need for disciplined standpoint-specification in assertion. cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.34-35

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the present project state, no school-defining Vaiyākaraṇa primary on disk treats `syād-vāda` as a native doctrinal topic, and the term is not constitutive of the school’s own technical lexicon for this extraction pass.

translator_note: "Conditional predication" is safer than "maybe-doctrine." The lazy rendering of *syāt* as "perhaps" imports epistemic uncertainty and makes the system sound skeptical or probabilistic. In the Jaina technical register, *syāt* marks standpoint-conditioned assertion, not hesitation.
